flags.location_quote: relating to the underlying order denying post- conviction relief, it will be treated as another postconviction relief application

The source PDF has 'post-' at the end of line 49 and 'conviction' at the start of line 50. This is NOT being proposed as a fix because it preserves the source's actual line-break structure, even though pdftotext rendered the joining awkwardly. The inconsistency (same word later appears as solid 'postconviction') exists in the source PDF itself.

coverage_note: Systematic comparison of DB text (43 lines, 2658 chars) against PDF-extracted text (69 lines, 2970 chars) via pdftotext. State of North Dakota,

v.

Alfred John Lizotte,

Plaintiff and Appellee

Defendant and Appellant

No. 20250226
flags.description: The DB caption lists parties and their roles in a spatially ambiguous structure: both party names appear, then both role designations on separate lines below. This creates semantic ambiguity about which role applies to which party. The authoritative PDF shows the correct legal caption format with roles paired directly with their parties on the same line: 'State of North Dakota, Plaintiff and Appellee' / 'v.' / 'Alfred John Lizotte, Defendant and Appellant'. The DB structure should be: State of ND paired with Plaintiff/Appellee role, and Lizotte paired with Defendant/Appellant role. This is a structural ordering issue in the caption, not a text transcription error. Flagged rather than fixed due to high-risk nature of party-name restructuring.

proposals.old_exact:
IV

in concluding Chapter 12.1-19.1

is

[¶147] The district court erred unconstitutionally vague
proposals.new_exact:
IV

[¶147] The district court erred in concluding Chapter 12.1-19.1 is unconstitutionally vague
proposals.evidence: The DB text has an errant paragraph break that separates 'in concluding Chapter 12.1-19.1' and 'is' onto orphan lines between section IV header and paragraph 147. These orphaned text fragments belong inside paragraph 147's opening sentence. The PDF source clearly shows: '[¶147] The district court erred in concluding Chapter 12.1-19.1 is unconstitutionally vague and further erred...' The DB version reads '[¶147] The district court erred unconstitutionally vague...' with the critical phrase 'in concluding Chapter 12.1-19.1 is' misplaced as orphan lines above the paragraph marker. This is an OCR/ingestion artifact where a page break or column break caused text to be reordered.
proposals.note: High-risk grammatical correction: The sentence is incomplete/unintelligible without the phrase 'in concluding Chapter 12.1-19.1 is'. The DB version creates a sentence fragment ('The district court erred unconstitutionally vague') that violates basic English grammar. The fix restores the complete, grammatical sentence as it appears in the authoritative PDF source. The replacement removes only 2 bytes net (due to collapsing orphaned lines and paragraph breaks).

flags.location_quote: Section IV, between section header and [¶147]

proposals.new_exact: I

proposals.old_exact: Ш

proposals.evidence: Section header error. The DB contains Cyrillic Sha (Ш U+0428, UTF-8: d0a8) which should be Roman numeral III (I+I+I). This appears at line 70 as section divider. Byte analysis confirms one occurrence. OCR rendering showed 'Il' (two capital I's) which OCR engine misread as Cyrillic. Proper section sequence: I, II, III, IV.
proposals.note: Precedes ¶11 discussing legality of sentences. Restores proper Roman numeral III section header.

proposals.old_exact: (alteration in original). “Detailed Id. including credibility determinations and references to evidence the court relied on in making its decision, inform the committed individual and this Court of the evidentiary basis for the court’s decision.” Id. findings,
proposals.new_exact: Id. (alteration in original). “Detailed findings, including credibility determinations and references to evidence the court relied on in making its decision, inform the committed individual and this Court of the evidentiary basis for the court’s decision.” Id.
proposals.source: PDF source (pdftotext from 2025ND164.pdf); confirmed via direct comparison with authoritative court PDF
proposals.evidence: Paragraph [¶9] contains a quote that was incorrectly processed during ingestion. The DB version has the text split and reordered: '(alteration in original). "Detailed Id. including...decision." Id. findings,'. The correct version per the PDF is: 'Id. (alteration in original). "Detailed findings, including...decision." Id.'. This error affects the sentence structure and meaning. The changes are: (1) move 'Id.' from after the closing quote to before the parenthetical; (2) change 'Detailed Id.' to 'Detailed findings,' within the quote; (3) change ' Id. findings,' to ' Id.' at the end. Both strings are exactly 260 characters, enabling a byte-for-byte replacement.
proposals.note: This is a significant structural error in how the quoted material is formatted and attributed. The parenthetical citation reference '(alteration in original)' should follow 'Id.', not precede the quote.

flags.location_quote: Sections I, II, III, then V (IV is skipped)
flags.description: Opinion sections jump from Roman numeral III directly to V. This is not an error—the opinion intentionally has no section IV, as confirmed by the PDF source. The gap is legitimate to the court's organizational structure.

The phrase 'Mr. Hoff cannot control his impulses, even if [sic] simple tasks' appears with the [sic] notation in both DB and PDF. This appears unusual and likely represents either a typo in the original court order being quoted or an intentional marking. Since both sources match and the [sic] is present in the authoritative PDF, this should not be changed. The sentence structure is awkward in the original court's findings being quoted, and the [sic] indicates awareness of this oddity.
